articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to authorize the Select Board, during Fiscal Year 2027, to acquire on behalf of the Town any and all easements for any of the following purposes: roads, sidewalks,vehicular and/or pedestrian access or passage, drainage and utilities, provided however that such authorization pertains only to easements acquired at no cost to the Town; and, further, to authorize the Select Board, subsequent to a public hearing, during Fiscal Year 2027 to abandon or relocate easements acquired for any of the foregoing purposes; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: The committee discussed that this is the standard recurring Article, and reminded everyone that it applies to actions undertaken at no cost to the Town.
articles.motion_text: MOVE that the Town vote to authorize the Select Board, during Fiscal Year 2027, to acquire on behalf of the Town any and all easements for any of the following purposes: roads, sidewalks,vehicular and/or pedestrian access or passage, drainage and utilities, provided however that such authorization pertains only to easements acquired at no cost to the Town; and, further, to authorize the Select Board, subsequent to a public hearing, during Fiscal Year 2027 to abandon or relocate easements acquired for any of the foregoing purposes.

articles.purpose: To authorize the Select Board, during Fiscal Year 2027, to acquire on behalf of the Town any and all easements for any of the following purposes: roads, sidewalks,vehicular and/or pedestrian access or passage, drainage and utilities, provided however that such authorization pertains only to easements acquired at no cost to the Town; and, further, to authorize the Select Board, subsequent to a public hearing, during Fiscal Year 2027 to abandon or relocate easements acquired for any of the foregoing purposes.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to hear and discuss the reports of town officers, boards, and committees; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Ms. Kate Mora, Chair of the Natick Net Zero Committee, presented the committee status update and report. The Net Zero Committee requested that the Motion be “To hear” rather than “To hear and discuss” the report – indicating that the committee’s preference is for people having questions or input to attend the committee’s regular meetings, where there’s better opportunities to discuss the wide range of matters Natick Net Zero works on. Some Finance Committee members spoke of respecting Town Meeting members’ time by not incurring unrequested debate. Other Finance Committee members opined that the policies that are or might be related to the Net Zero initiative are very consequential, and that not discussing the matter at Town Meeting may be seen as a missed opportunity for broad citizen engagement. The Finance Committee extensively discussed the pros and cons of the two options, and voted to support the Net Zero Committee’s requested motion language.
articles.motion_text: MOVE to hear the report of the Natick Net Zero Committee.
open_items: Confirm parsed recommendation, vote quantum, motion, and discussion against the official PDF.
articles.purpose: To hear and discuss the reports of town officers, boards, and committees.
articles.title: Committee Article

articles.article_language: Town To determine what sum of money the Town will appropriate and raise, transfer from available funds for the operation of the government of the Town of Natick, including debt and interest, during Fiscal Year 2026 (July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2026) and to provide for reserve fund for Fiscal Year 2026 and to see what budgets for Fiscal Year 2026 will be reduced to offset said additional appropriations; To determine what sum of money the Town will appropriate and raise, or transfer from available funds for the operation of the government of the Town of Natick, including debt and interest, during Fiscal Year 2027 (July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2027) and to provide for reserve funds for Fiscal Year 2027; Libraries To determine what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, or otherwise provide, for the maintenance and operation of the Morse Institute Library and the Bacon Free Library, for Fiscal Year 2026 (July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2026); To determine what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, or otherwise provide, for the maintenance and operation of the Morse Institute Library and the Bacon Free Library, for Fiscal Year 2027 (July 1, 2026, through June 30, 2027); or otherwise act thereon
articles.discussion: The detailed Departmental Line-Item budgets and supporting narratives are included in the Town Administrator’s Revised FY2027 Budget Book, and in the Natick Public Schools’ FY2027 Budget Book. These budget books are critical companion documents to this Finance Committee Recommendation book – and have been separately mailed to each Town Meeting Member. The overview of the Natick Public Schools’ and Town Libraries’ hearing with the Education & Learning Subcommittee was presented by Richard Pope, Chair. Superintendent Melissa Spash, Matt Gillis, Assistant Superintendent for Finance, and Erin Miller, Director of Student Services were available to answer additional questions. Natick Public Schools Collective Bargaining Agreements and Transportation are the main cost drivers. Fee increases went into effect for many programs including after school care, activities. The subcommittee met twice for the NPS budget, which is largely a level-services budget. A committee member asked about last year’s turnbacks by department referenced in the budget book, which was in the $1,000 - $2,000 range, which is quite tight to the $100,000,000+ budget. Mr. Gillis explained that they attempt to budget very close to expected actuals, and coupled with reclassifications during the prior fiscal year, the spending came in very close to the appropriation. A member asked about the reclassification of some staff from specialists to in-classroom. Dr. Spash confirmed that the reclassification table provided in the NPS Budget Book is accurate and complete, and was reviewed in detail with the new Director of HR. Morse Institute Library & Bacon Free Library Statewide requirements for increasing library budgets are becoming more challenging for municipalities, but Natick is remaining compliant. A new Director of the Bacon Free Library has just started their tenure after the previous Director’s retirement. The subcommittee confirmed that the Bacon Free Library’s budget is accurate with respect to the new Director’s position. Town of Natick Jamie Errickson, Natick Town Administrator, Ari Sky, Deputy Town Administrator / Finance Director, and Gloria Huang, Budget and Financial Analyst, provided support for Article 3. There were some updates from the preliminary budget that was presented in February, and these changes are incorporated into these motions, and the Revised Town Administrator’s FY27 Budget Book. Fincom member David Coffey, Chair of the General Government Subcommittee, reviewed their discussions. 52 The Information Technology budget was discussed at length, and an additional full-time position may be requested in the future. Mr. Errickson mentioned that there is a new Information Technology position that will be split 60/40% with the Schools; there was a retirement on the Town’s side, so additional funds were not required to fund the new position. A committee member expressed continuing concerns over the spend on certain IT infrastructure. Mr, Errickson explained that years of under-budgeting on the Town side has created some deficiencies that need addressing over several years – and that there may be future budgets that also need to continue catching up. The Public Works budget was amended from the preliminary budget to include $100,000 to support the DPW safety training to meet OSHA compliance (municipalities were previously exempt). The DPW funding will need to address the current $2,500,000 in snow removal overages. There is sufficient flexibility to manage that overage, and the committee reviewed the state rules and guidance on how Snow & Ice budgeting works. The Town Clerk budget is impacted by three elections this year - the Town Election, State Primary, and November Election. The Fire Department is anticipating increased Assessment Center costs for filling senior positions in the next year. The Town did not yet have plans to request funds for the Recreation & Parks Department to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution or have community events for Independence Day. There may be opportunities to partner with community groups, providing limited support of independent events. Several members expressed concerns regarding the current general environment of fiscal uncertainty. Town Administration has increased the Finance Committee Reserve to provide some additional reserve capacity. As in prior years, unused Reserves will drop to Free Cash. The West Suburban Health Group, which negotiates the health insurance benefits for the employees of the Town, came in under the preliminary budget by $140,000, and those funds are being redeployed as seen in this amended budget. The Town is budgeting additional funds to the Water & Sewer Enterprise Reserve Fund in anticipation of MWRA connection expenses and future capital projects. A Member expressed dissatisfaction with the requested appropriation under Motion D - Replacement of Police Badges & Uniforms; $120,000. A significant portion of this request is due to the prior change of the Town Seal. The member feels that it was originally expected that these expenses would happen as natural wear and tear caused uniforms to be replaced.

articles.article_language: General Stabilization To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds or otherwise provide for the purpose of supplementing the Stabilization Fund established under Article 22 of the warrant for Annual Town Meeting of 1961, as authorized by Chapter 40,Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended; Operational Stabilization To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds or otherwise provide for the purpose of supplementing the Operational Stabilization Fund established by vote of the 2011 Spring Annual Town Meeting under Article 4, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended; Capital Stabilization To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds or otherwise provide for the purpose of supplementing the Capital Stabilization Fund established by vote of the 2010 Fall Annual Town Meeting under Article 2, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended; OPEB To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds or otherwise provide for, the Other Post-Employment Benefits Liability Trust Fund established by vote of the 2017 Spring Annual Town Meeting under Article 15, pursuant to the provisions of Chapter 32B, Section 20 of the General Laws as amended by section 15 of Chapter 218 of the Acts of 2016; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Ari Sky, Deputy Town Administrator/Finance Director of the Town of Natick presented the article. He shared that there was some redistribution of Free Cash and Capital projects, so the bottom line has not changed from earlier drafts. A list of the current funds and balances was also shared: 55 A member asked about the FAR and I&I funds, and referenced that those are truly restricted funds. He asked if we invest these into the Mass Municipal Depository Trust and if there was interest. Mr. Sky confirmed they are invested and there is interest. The member then asked the projected gross liability for OPEB Trust. Mr. Sky said the most recent study said about $151 million. Per the town’s financial management principles and policies, the strategy has been to satisfy the pension liability and then once complete in 2030-31 move towards the OPEB liability. A member asked the presenter to give an overview of each fund. Mr. Skyi shared that the Capital Stabilization fund exists to strengthen the town’s ability to fund capital projects. There is a formula for the minimum amount. This is an overfunding but we know we will have projects in the fall. The General Stabilization fund is for unforeseen or catastrophic events, its goal and contribution is formula based. Operations/Rainy Day Stabilization is for economic downturn/service gap coverage. OPEB is for Non Retiree Health Benefits. FAR is Floor Area Ratio and used for open space and public parks. I & I Stabilization stands for Inflow and Infiltration and is for our Water and Sewer work (repair sewer lines), and 1:1 Technology Stabilization is a high school technology equipment program. A member asked about the health of these funds and if we met all the requirements for minimums on all the funds. Mr. Sky said yes, we are actually exceeding for Capital as mentioned. The member then said from a Covid perspective, asked if we regained what we used, or if there are targets we want to hit in the future. Mr. Sky said we are complying with the financial management principles Natick has established, but the Select Board/Administration have expressed a desire to re-examine and update the financial management principles. Mr. Errickson added that we have not yet met the pre-COVID balances of many of these accounts, though we are on target (primarily Capital and General stabilization are not yet back to pre pandemic levels) A member asked about the cost of snow removal and whether any of these funds would go to that. Mr. Sky said there are a few options for the Town regarding covering the snow removal expense for 2026. The town is currently at a $2,000,000+ deficit in the previously allocated snow removal budget; there are different ways to address the funding gap - through some re- allocations within the Department of Public Works budget, and if needed, a portion of Free Cash. The member clarified that Free Cash is rarely certified before Fall Town Meeting and whether we need to use any of these funds before then. Mr. Sky said they do not anticipate needing to use these funds. A member asked about the 3 restricted funds - FAR Stabilization, I & I Stabilization Fund, and 1:1 Tech Stabilization - is the 1:1 Tech being actively used? Is there a way to close it out or re-allocate it? The town confirmed that this fund has not been used for a while. The member encouraged the town to explore how either it can be used or close out the account. The member continued and asked for FAR and I & I, and if they have indefinite purposes, is interest added to the funds? Mr. Sky said yes, they all go into the respective fund.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to appropriate $6,924,290 from FY 2026 Certified Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Town’s Stabilization Funds and OPEB Trust Fund as follows: ● $5,206,342 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Capital Stabilization Fund established by the vote of Article 2 of the 2010 Fall Annual Town Meeting, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. ● $1,127,170 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the General Stabilization Fund established under Article 22 of the warrant for Annual Town Meeting of 1961, as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. ● $26,668 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the Operational/Rainy Day Stabilization Fund established under Article 4 of the 2011 Spring Annual Town Meeting as authorized by Chapter 40, Section 5B of the General Laws, as amended. ● $564,110 from Free Cash for the purpose of supplementing the OPEB Trust Fund established under Article 15 of the 2017 Spring Annual Town Meeting, as authorized by Chapter 32B, Section 20 of the General Laws, as amended by Section 15 of Chapter 218 of the Acts of 2016.

articles.article_language: To see what sum of money the Town will vote to appropriate from the PEG Access and Cable Related Fund, established by vote of 2019 Special Town Meeting #1 under Article 1, as authorized by Chapter 44, Section 53F 3/4 of the General Laws, as amended, to fund PEG access programming, as well as certain other municipal cable related expenses; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Mr. Ari Sky, Deputy Town Administrator / Finance Director presented this article. This is the standard semi-annual appropriation to fund Natick Pegasus from fees collected from cable subscribers and remitted to the town by the three cable license holders operating in Natick. The committee discussed the need for planning alternative funding sources for Pegasus, since the hardwired cable subscription model is declining as streaming becomes more popular.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend Article 41A (Revolving Funds) of the Natick Town Bylaws as provided for under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, section 53E 1/2; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Jon Marshall, Deputy Administrator of Operations, presented this Article 7, which is a cleanup and rewrite of the existing bylaw on town revolving funds, Bylaw 41A. It does not create any new revolving funds or authorize any expenditures (companion Article 8 will authorize expenditures from the revolving funds). Review of existing Bylaw 41A was prompted by the establishment of the new stormwater revolving fund by fall 2025 town meeting. This rewrite is based on a model bylaw provided by the state Department of Revenue's Division of Municipal Services (DLS). The primary changes are to add a Purpose section, to revise the description of the annual reporting on each revolving fund, and to move the description of the various funds and purposes into a table. In addition, the revision consistently identifies a department or board (rather than a particular officer of a department or board) as the entity authorized to spend each fund. A FinCom member noted that the table includes a column for restrictions or conditions on expenses payable from each fund and that this column is empty. Mr. Marshall noted that several other communities’ revolving fund Bylaws were reviewed for restrictions and found to have none. Even so, the column (which exists in the DLS model bylaw) was retained in case the town were to add restrictions in the future. A FinCom member asked why the new Bylaw directs income from each fund to the general fund rather than be retained in the particular revolving fund. It was noted that this is a requirement of Massachusetts General Law Chapter 44,Section 53E½, the state law that permits towns to establish revolving funds. A FinCom member asked if the changes to the reporting section would mean that existing reporting on the revolving funds would no longer be available. Mr. Marshall indicated that the changes were semantic and not material - that no changes to the content of existing reports were intended. FinCom voted unanimously to recommend that Town Meeting approve the motion for Article 7 as written.
articles.motion_text: Move to amend Article 41A (Revolving Funds) of the Natick Town Bylaws as provided for under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, section 53E ½, by striking the existing language, and replacing it with the following: Section 1. Purpose This by-law establishes and authorizes revolving funds for use by Town departments, boards, committees, agencies or officers in connection with the operation of programs or activities that generate fees, charges or other receipts to support all or some of the expenses of those programs or activities. These revolving funds are established under and governed by General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2 Section 2. Expenditure Limitations Liabilities may be incurred against and funds may be expended from a revolving fund established and authorized by this by-law without appropriation subject to the following limitations: a) Fringe benefits of full time employees whose salaries or wages are paid from the fund shall also be paid from the fund; 59 b) No liability shall be incurred in excess of the available balance of the fund; and, c) In any fiscal year, the limit on the amount that may be spent from a revolving fund may be increased with the approval of the Select Board and finance committee. Section 3. Interest Interest earned on the monies credited to a revolving fund established by this by-law shall be credited to the general fund. Section 4. Procedures and Reports Except as provided in General Laws Chapter 44, Section 53E1/2 and this by-law, the laws, charter provisions, by-laws, rules, regulations, policies or procedures that govern the receipt and custody of town’s monies and the expenditure and payment of town funds shall apply to the use of a revolving fund established and authorized by the is by-law. The town comptroller shall include a statement on the collections credited to each fund, the encumbrances and expenditures charged to the fund and the balance available for expenditure in the regular report the town comptroller provides the department, board, committee, agency or officer on appropriations made for its use. Section 5. Authorized Revolving Funds The table establishes: A. Each revolving fund authorized for use by a town department, board, committee, agency or officer, B. The department or agency head, board, committee or officer authorized to spend from each fund, C. The fees, charges and other monies charged and received by the department, board, committee, agency or officer in connection with the program or activity for which the fund is established that shall be credited to each fund by the town comptroller, D. The expenses of the program or activity for which each fund may be used, E. Any restriction or condition on expenditures from each fund, F. Any reporting or other requirements that apply to each fund, and G. The fiscal years each fund shall operate under this by-law. 60 61 ***END OF MOTION*** 62
open_items: Confirm parsed recommendation, vote quantum, motion, and discussion against the official PDF.
articles.purpose: To amend Article 41A (Revolving Funds) of the Natick Town Bylaws as provided for under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, section 53E 1/2; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.title: Amend Bylaw Article 41A (Revolving Funds)

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote on the limit of the total amount that may be expended from each revolving fund established pursuant to Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, section 53E ½ and Article 41A (Revolving Funds) of the Natick Town Bylaws; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Revolving Funds: Balances as of 3/17/2026 65 Jon Marshall, Deputy Town Administrator, presented Article 8, which sets the annual spending limits for each of the Town's revolving funds as required by General Laws, Chapter 44, Section 53E½. He also presented the above table showing the FY27 proposed spending limit, the FY26 limit, the fund balance at the start of FY26, the expenditures (if any) for FY26 to date, and the balance to date (3/17/26, the date of the table, 2 days prior to the FinCom hearing). All but 4 funds are proposed to be set at the same limit as for fiscal year 2026. The four that are different are: (1) DPW Surplus Vehicle Fund, set at $500,000, an increase of $150,000, (2) Community - Senior Center Programs, set at $175,000, an increase of $55,000, (3) Recreation and Parks, set at $2,250,000, an increase of $250,000, and (4) Stormwater, set at $75,000 (this is a new fund, established by fall 2025 Town Meeting, and therefore has never before has spending). Note that spending may not exceed the voted limit, but also may not exceed the balance in the particular fund (for example, the newest fund, Stormwater, currently has no balance and may not have any spending until there is a balance, regardless of the voted limit.) A Fincom member asked what is driving the increases for those funds which increased. Mr. Marshall offered that: ● for DPW Surplus Vehicles, the balance had continued to grow. It is funded by receipts from the sales of surplus vehicles. It is used both to replace vehicles on a planned basis, but also to cover repairs or replacements of vehicles that are in accidents (insurance typically does not cover the full replacement value). ● for Community - Senior Center Programs, the fund is funded by building rental fees and program fees. Based on anticipated activity the fund limit is proposed to be modestly increased. ● for Recreation and Parks, this is a large fund, funded by program fees and costs for activities. Among the upcoming large expenditures for this fund is to pay a portion of the costs of the replacement of Cole Field which has reached the end of its useful life. The fund could not cover the full cost of field replacement, so another Town Meeting appropriation will be expected for the balance. ● for Stormwater, the increase is simply because it is a new fund which has never before had funding or a limit. A FinCom member asked if any additional authorization was required from these funds after limits were established. It was noted that the limits are the sole authorization needed and that once a limit was established the fund could be spent without further authorization (up to the limit) for the fiscal year for any of the fund's authorized purposes. FinCom voted unanimously to recommend that Town Meeting approve the motion for Article 8 as written.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to authorize the following maximum expenditures for FY 2027 for the listed revolving funds, established by the Town of Natick Bylaws, Article 41 A pursuant to Chapter 44, §53E 1⁄2 of the Massachusetts General Laws, as follows: Revolving Fund Department, Board, Committee, Agency or Officer Authorized to Spend from Fund FY 2027 Spending Limit DPW Vehicles and Equipment Public Works $500,000 Morse Institute Library Purchase of New Books and Related Materials Morse Institute Library $85,000 Morse Institute Library Maintenance and Repair of Library Facilities and Equipment Morse Institute Library $25,000 Community-Senior Center Rental, Maintenance and Improvement Projects Community Services $75,000 Flu Clinics, Immunization Programs, Pandemic and Emergency Preparedness Board of Health $60,000 Community-Senior Center Programs and Activities Community Services $175,000 64 Tobacco Control Programs and Enforcement Board of Health $25,000 Tax Title Takings or Tax Title Foreclosure Finance $100,000 Curbside Compost Collection Program Sustainability $20,000 Community Gardens Community Services $30,000 Energy Efficiency and Clean Energy Capital Program Sustainability $300,000 Recreation and Parks Programs Community Services $2,250,000 Cochituate Rail Trail Maintenance and Enhancements Public Works $75,000 Stormwater Fund Community and Economic Development $75,000

articles.article_language: To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, borrow, transfer from available funds or otherwise provide to implement a Capital Improvement Program as may be required for capital equipment for the various departments of the Town of Natick; to protect the physical infrastructure of the Town of Natick, to add new physical infrastructure, or to improve community assets; and further to determine whether this appropriation shall be raised by borrowing or otherwise; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Deputy Town Administrators Jon Marshall (Operations) and Ari Sky (Finance) presented this article. They reviewed and discussed the updated process for prioritization of Capital projects and requests, including a review of the considerations of debt service projections and management. The Finance Committee members discussed the various Spring 2026 appropriation requests, as well as the updated Capital project review and coordination process. Committee members and members of the public received an overview of the interaction of Capital planning and Debt management. In addition to the process of prioritization of Capital project according to ever-changing needs and the capacity of town staff to manage the projects, Debt Service projections play a major part of appropriation timing. Members questioned and discussed the current economic uncertainties, and the impact on projections. The Sponsors shared those concerns, which are incorporated into the Spring request and will further impact considerations of what’s brought forward in the Fall, Members asked about the new Tiers, and how that process is structured. In general, potential Capital Projects are assessed on criticality, readiness to proceed, project manage capacity, and ability to fund - either via available cash, or borrowing capacity. Members questioned 2 Tiers vs 3 (or more), and it was explained that 2 Tiers simplify presentations, and that the underlying categorization is more detailed. Members also reviewed the process that reviews remaining useful life vs accelerating maintenance/repair costs, and Mr Bill Spratt explained the evaluations, especially with regard to the vehicle replacement program. Members also questioned other potential funding sources, such as utilizing certain Water & Sewer stabilization funds. It was explained that none of the Spring 2026 projects are eligible for those funds. 72 73 74 75 76 77 78 79 80 81 82 83 84 85 86 87 88 89
articles.motion_text: A (Requires a 2/3 Vote) Motion A: Move that the Town vote to appropriate the sum of $3,950,000 to be expended as follows: • Under the direction of the Public Works Department for the purpose of: • Vehicle & Equipment Replacement Program • 10 Wheel Truck/Sander • Sanitation Large Swaploader • Recycling Truck 67 • Under the direction of the Public Works Department (Water/Sewer) for the purpose of: • Evergreen Well #3 • H&T Greensand Vessel • Vehicle & Equipment Replacement Program • Vactor Truck all individually shown as items 1 through 4 in Table A below, and that to meet this appropriation the Treasurer with the approval of the Select Board is authorized to borrow $3,950,000 under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 44, Section 7, as amended, or any other enabling authority and to issue bonds or notes of the Town therefore aggregating not more than $3,950,000 in principal amount and that the Town Administrator, with the approval of the Select Board, is authorized to take any action necessary to carry out this program, and further, that any premium received by the Town upon the sale of any bonds or notes approved by this vote, less any such premium applied to the payment of the costs of issuance of such bonds or notes, may be applied to the payment of costs approved by this vote in accordance with Chapter 44, Section 20 of the General Laws, thereby reducing the amount authorized to be borrowed to pay such costs by a like amount. articles.article_language: To see if the Town, pursuant to the authority contained in Section 108A of Chapter 41 of the General Laws, will vote to amend Article 24 of the Natick Town Bylaws, specifically the Classification and Pay Plan referenced in Section 3, paragraph 3.10 therein, by adding, deleting or amending position titles; re-classifying positions to a different Grade; and/or effecting changes in the salary ranges as presently established; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Mr. Steve Levinsky, Chair of the Personnel Board presented the Article to the Finance Committee. He reviewed the updates, and reminded the committee that this article is updating job titles and grades – but makes no changes to pay ranges at this time, and does not appropriate or spend funds. In several cases, there have been updates to certain job descriptions. Finance Committee members review and discussed some of the modifications, with Mr. Levinsky assisting in tracking how specific positions moved through the matrix of grades and title modifications. A member requested additional info on IT positions (IT Manager vs Network & Systems Engineer) and details on position Grades within the department. Town Meeting members can review the Memo from the Personnel Board for additional overview of the changes (attached). 96 Memorandum TO: Town Meeting Select Board Finance Committee Town Administrator CC: Personnel Board Director of Human Resources FROM: Steve Levinsky, Chair of Personnel Board DATE: March 16, 2026 RE: Revised Classification and Pay Plan - 2026 Spring Annual Town Meeting _____________________________________________________________________ Per Article 24 of the Town Bylaws, the Personnel Board is responsible to “review and recommend a Classification and Pay Plan adjustments to be presented to the Finance Committee and Town Meeting”. Attached is the proposed revised Classification and Pay Plan for consideration at 2026 Spring Annual Town Meeting, as reviewed and recommended by the Personnel Board. For ease of reading, also included is a redlined version showing changes from the most recently reviewed plan presented at the 2025 Spring Annual Town Meeting. This plan applies to the Town’s general government non-union employees, representing approximately 85 full-time positions (about 1/5th of the Town (non-school) personnel). The Classification and Pay Plan establishes the Town’s compensation structure for these positions, including salary grades and ranges assigned to each classification. The Plan also identifies the positions covered and their corresponding job classifications. Job descriptions for these positions are available on the Town’s website. The Classification and Pay Plan provides the framework for compensation administration. Appropriations for individual departments and employees are addressed through separate warrant articles within the annual operating budget. The determination of individual employee salaries within the established pay ranges, consistent with applicable job descriptions, remains the responsibility of the Town Administrator. This warrant article does not request an appropriation of funds and does not create or authorize additional positions. 1 97 Changes to the Full-Time Pay Plan: Grade 1 ● Community Outreach Coordinator added to reﬂect staﬃng at the Morse Institute Library. This is not an addition to the headcount at the Morse. Grade 2 ● Human Resources Coordinator retitled to Human Resources Specialist to align with industry standards. Grade 3 ● Assistant Director – Programs retitled to Program Manager – Older Adult Programs to align with industry standards. ● Assistant Director – Programs & Special Events retitled to Program Manager – Camps & Y outh Programs. ● Assistant Director – Resource Allocations retitled to Business & Operations Manager. This position assumes the primary responsibilities previously associated with resource allocation, including scheduling community service facilities and rentals, scheduling building monitors, and supervising administrative staff supporting Recreation & Parks, Council on Aging, and Community Services budgets. ● Business & Special Projects Manager replaced with Director of Business and Operations noted below. ● Information Technology Manager retitled to Network & Systems Engineer to accurately reﬂect the current duties and responsibilities of the position to align the title with the role. ● Senior Environmental Health Specialist retitled to Assistant Director of Public Health to enhance coordination, accountability, and responsiveness in addressing the Town’s public health needs. ● Veteran’s Agent retitled to Veterans Service Oﬃcer to align with terminology used by the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. Grade 4 ● Director of Business and Operations position added. This position was created by combining two positions: Director of Public Works (Grade 5) and Business & Special Projects Manager (Grade 3). Responsibilities from both positions were consolidated into this role. This consolidation is expected to generate departmental savings and shift 2 98 certain financial and administrative responsibilities away from divisional supervisors, allowing them to focus more directly on operational management. Grade 5 ● Assistant Fire Chief added. Funding for this position was previously approved by Town Meeting (FY25 and FY26). Creation of this role strengthens departmental management capacity and supports succession planning within the Fire Department. ● Director of Public Works replaced with Director of Business and Operations noted above. Grade 6 ● No changes. Changes to the Part - Time Pay Plan: Grade 1 ● Leader/Counselor Certified (Rec) replaced with Counselor (Rec ) Position added. ● Senior Counselor Certified (Rec) replaced with Head Counselor (Rec) Grade 2 ● Assistant Director (Rec) retitled to Assistant Director – Camps/Programs (Rec) to align with industry standards. ● Camp Director - Title updated to include “(Rec)” to support Recreation & Park programs. ● Program Assistant (CS) - Title updated to include “(CS)” to provide broader support for community service programs. ● Program Supervisor - Title updated to remove “(Rec)” from the title to allow broader use of this position within Community Services. ● Water Safety Instructor added to support Recreation & Parks programming. Grade 3 ● Community Liaison added. This is a part-time position which will support Natick’s Municipal Vulnerability Preparedness (MVP) 2.0 Core Team. The MVP program, administered by the Massachusetts Executive Oﬃce of Energy and Environmental Affairs, provides grant funding to help municipalities identify climate vulnerabilities and invest in resilience planning and implementation. 3 99 ● Instructor IV added to support Community Services programming. Helpful Resource Links: All full-time non-union job descriptions are available on the Town’s website: https://www.natickma.gov/285/Non-Union-Full-Time-Job-Descriptions Past Personnel Board agendas and meeting minutes are available at: https://www.natickma.gov/AgendaCenter 4 100

Asterisk and Strike-through notations will be removed prior to inclusion by reference into Article 24, Section 3, Paragraph 3.10.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to authorize the Select Board to file Special Legislation with the General Court under the Home Rule Amendment to the Massachusetts Constitution or take any other action thereon, said Special Legislation to read as follows; provided, however, that the General Court may make clerical or editorial changes of form only to the bill, unless the Select Board approves amendments thereto before enactment by the General Court, which amendments shall be within the public purposes of said petition; or otherwise act thereon. AN ACT AUTHORIZING THE TOWN OF NATICK TO CONTINUE THE EMPLOYMENT OF POLICE CHIEF JAMES HICKS BEYOND THE AGE OF 65. SECTION I. Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, James Hicks, Police Chief of the Town of Natick, may continue to serve in that position until reaching age 67, the date of his retirement, or the date he is relieved of his duties by the Select Board at their discretion, whichever occurs first; provided, however, that he is mentally and physically capable of performing the duties of Police Chief. The Select Board may, at the Town's own expense, require that James Hicks be examined annually by a physician designated by the Select Board, to determine such physical and mental capability to perform the duties of his office. No further deductions shall be made from the regular compensation of James Hicks, pursuant to chapter 32 of the General Laws for service subsequent to his reaching age 65; and upon retirement for superannuation, he shall receive a superannuation retirement allowance equal to the allowance that he would have been entitled had he retired upon reaching age 65
articles.discussion: The article was presented in the Finance Committee Hearing on March 12 by Bruce Evans of the Select Board and Chief James Hicks. The police chief and the deputy chief are subject to mandatory retirement statutes this year. For each of them to continue in his respective role into 2027 will require successful passage of enabling home rule petition legislation by the state legislature. Article 12 is the article to enable the home rule petition for the chief’s continued employment. It was stated that the average police force tenure is not historically high and that the supervisory experience on the force is generally light, especially with the imminent retirements of two lieutenants and one sergeant. It was stated that tight staffing currently for rank and file officers makes it difficult to promote officers needed for street duty. Attempts to hire mid-career employees with more experience have so far not proven productive. Extending the chief’s tenure would let him see through the completion of a regional dispatch project currently underway, and would create time to develop succession options. A Finance Committee member asked if other municipalities commonly extend contracts beyond the statutory retirement age. Chief Hicks responded that two communities are known to have done so. A Finance Committee member asked if alternative arrangements might be considered involving consulting or project-based engagement without an extended contract. Mr. Evans indicated some openness to considering flexible or creative alternative options in parallel with pursuing the home rule petition. A Finance Committee member noted that the subject is being raised close to the deadline for the action it contemplates, when the facts of the chief’s age have been known and could have prompted action sooner. A Finance Committee member noted that among the points made in support when Town Meeting acted on the Deputy Chief position was that it could facilitate succession planning, and yet the current configuration does not facilitate succession without the home rule petitions. The presenters responded that while the intention of the Deputy Chief position included succession planning, that intention depended on the applicant pool for the deputy position supporting the intention. It was also noted that some of the recent or imminent retirements were unexpected. A motion was made and seconded to recommend favorable action on the motion for article 12 as presented. The vote was 6-4-0. The affirmative vote did not reach the quantum of 8 votes required for a Finance Committee recommendation.

AN ACT AUTHORIZING THE TOWN OF NATICK TO CONTINUE THE EMPLOYMENT OF POLICE CHIEF JAMES HICKS. SECTION I. Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, James Hicks, Police Chief of the Town of Natick, may continue to serve in that position until reaching age 67, the date of his retirement, or the date he is relieved of his duties by the Select Board at their discretion, whichever occurs first; provided, however, that he is mentally and physically capable of performing the duties of Police Chief. The Select Board may, at the Town's own expense, require that James Hicks be examined annually by a physician designated by the Select Board, to determine such physical and mental capability to perform the duties of his office. 102 No further deductions shall be made from the regular compensation of James Hicks, pursuant to chapter 32 of the General Laws for service subsequent to his reaching age 65; and upon retirement for superannuation, he shall receive a superannuation retirement allowance equal to the allowance that he would have been entitled had he retired upon reaching age 65.

AN ACT AUTHORIZING THE TOWN OF NATICK TO CONTINUE THE EMPLOYMENT OF POLICE CHIEF JAMES HICKS BEYOND THE AGE OF 65. 101 SECTION I. Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, James Hicks, Police Chief of the Town of Natick, may continue to serve in that position until reaching age 67, the date of his retirement, or the date he is relieved of his duties by the Select Board at their discretion, whichever occurs first; provided, however, that he is mentally and physically capable of performing the duties of Police Chief. The Select Board may, at the Town's own expense, require that James Hicks be examined annually by a physician designated by the Select Board, to determine such physical and mental capability to perform the duties of his office. articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to authorize the Select Board to file Special Legislation with the General Court under the Home Rule Amendment to the Massachusetts Constitution or take any other action thereon, said Special Legislation to read as follows; provided, however, that the General Court may make clerical or editorial changes of form only to the bill, unless the Select Board approves amendments thereto before enactment by the General Court, which amendments shall be within the public purposes of said petition; or take any other action relative thereto. AN ACT AUTHORIZING THE TOWN OF NATICK TO CONTINUE THE EMPLOYMENT OF DEPUTY POLICE CHIEF BRIAN LAUZON. SECTION I. Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, Brian Lauzon, Deputy Police Chief of the Town of Natick, may continue to serve in that position until reaching age 67, the date of his retirement, or the date he is relieved of his duties by the Select Board at their discretion, whichever occurs first; provided, however, that he is mentally and physically capable of performing the duties of Deputy Police Chief. The Select Board may, at the Town's own expense, require that Brian Lauzon be examined annually by a physician designated by the Select Board, to determine such physical and mental capability to perform the duties of his office. No further deductions shall be made from the regular compensation of Brian Lauzon, pursuant to chapter 32 of the General Laws for service subsequent to his reaching age 65; and upon retirement for superannuation, he shall receive a superannuation retirement allowance equal to the allowance that he would have been entitled had he retired upon reaching age 65.
articles.discussion: A member shared that when the Deputy Chief position was first envisioned, the justification was that it would aid in succession planning for when Chief Hicks was retiring. However, by hiring someone who was the same age as Chief Hicks, did we fail in our succession plan? Chief Hicks clarified it was about who applied. There were three applicants and only one was an internal candidate. One got a position in another department and the other applicant was an outside candidate who we felt was not adequate for the position, so it was a reflection of who applied. A member asked why we need two people to do this plan. Mr. Evans shared that the Select Board felt there were a lot of synergies between the current Chief and the Deputy Chief, similar to a head coach and an offensive coordinator. It’s important to have trust between both individuals so that when you are not around there is continuity. In addition, the Select Board realized that when they talked to the Chief and Deputy Chief individually, they had the same goals - there is unfinished work, they want to do it, and we want to leave the department in the best shape possible for the next individual(s). Motions and Debate A member moved to refer the motion, that was seconded. A move for favorable action did not receive a second. In debate, a member said the purpose of their referral recommendation is due to the succession plan. They shared that it might make sense for Chief Hicks to continue, but that a new Deputy Chief should be brought in and trained while the Chief continues, so that we can start preparing the next leader. Another member expressed continued discomfort with the long transition period and stated that this position does not warrant such an extension.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will amend Natick Town Bylaws Article 50, Section 16 (Animal Control); or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: The article was first presented at the Finance Committee hearing of March 10 by the Animal Control Officer and the Town Administrator. Finance Committee members noted that there were very significant semantic and syntactical deficiencies in the motion as presented. The committee unanimously voted to Refer the article back to the sponsor for additional work. The administration provided a revised motion, and the article was reconsidered on April 7th. Chief Hicks appeared to respond to any questions that might arise. Members noted that the language of the revised motion supported the intention of the article. One Finance Committee member noted that a few minor grammatical issues remained, for example with consistent singular/plural agreement. While those issues are not fatal to the clarity of the motion’s meaning, they could be corrected to perfect the motion. The member noted that there had been some advice not to make the correction because some of the errors are present in the existing bylaw which had been reviewed by the Attorney General. The member opined that the Attorney General’s review is for constitutionality and compliance with law, and does not necessarily include blessing grammatical errors. While no amending motion was made during the hearing to correct any grammatical error, the member expressed hope that Town Meeting might take such action. A motion was made and seconded to recommend positive action on the motion as presented at the April 7 Finance Committee hearing. It passed unanimously, 12-0-0.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to amend Article 50, Section 16 as follows: ● Add a new sentence to the end of Section 16.7 Fines, that reads: “In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a dog owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer or Animal 107 Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section.” ● In Section 16.7, replace any gender specific pronouns with non-gender specific pronouns; and ● Remove “For the purpose of this section, pet shall be defined as follows” from the first line of Section 16.11; and ● Add a new definition to Section 16.11 for “Vaccinated” to be defined “as described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e.” So that new Article 50, Section 16.7 reads as follows: 16.7 Fines - If the investigating officer determines that a violation of this section has occurred, fines may be levied according to the following schedule: First offense $50.00 Second offense $75.00 Third and subsequent offenses $100.00 Unrestrained dog found on school or park property $100.00 Dog unlicensed $100.00 Personal Kennel unlicensed $500.00 Dog unvaccinated for rabies $300.00 Commercial Kennel unlicensed $500.00 In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137 and 138 of the Massachusetts General Laws, a dog owner shall license their dogs in the Town of Natick annually by March 1, and shall pay the required license fee. Any dog owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 per dog in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to Chapter 140, Sections 137A of the Massachusetts General Laws, a person maintaining a kennel shall license their kennel annually by March 1st, and shall pay the required license fee. Any kennel owner who fails to do so shall pay to the Town of Natick a fine in the amount of $50.00 in addition to the annual license fee as pursuant to Chapter 140, Section 141 of the Massachusetts General Laws. In addition to the foregoing fines, pursuant to M.G.L. Chapter 140, Section 145B, subsections a-e, a pet owner that cannot provide a certificate of rabies vaccination for their dog within 24 hours of when asked for by a Police Officer, Animal Control Officer 108 or Animal Inspector, said dog shall be considered unvaccinated and the owner shall be subject to the dog unvaccinated for rabies fine noted in this section. And so that Article 50 Section 16.11 reads as follows: 16.11 Definitions: • Pet: any domesticated animal, not including livestock or fowl, which is commonly housed within or upon the owner's or keeper's premises. • Commercial Kennels: Either a “Commercial Boarding or Training Kennel”, or a “Commercial Breeder Kennel” as those terms are described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A; • Personal Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Domestic Charitable Kennels: As described in MGL Ch 140 Section 136A. • Vaccinated: As described in M.G.L. Chapter 140 s.145B, subsections a-e.
open_items: Confirm parsed recommendation, vote quantum, motion, and discussion against the official PDF.
articles.purpose: To amend Bylaw Article 50, Section 16.11 Definition by adding a new definition "Unvaccinated". This definition will create better clarity to the bylaw and provide an item that is currently not defined. Further, this places a time expectation when requesting proof of current vaccination against rabies. As is sometimes the case, a dog owner doesn't have current proof of rabies vaccination at the time of the bite, but says they have it at home. Typically, the Animal Control Officer will have to wait a week or more before receiving proof of rabies (or lack thereof) for the dog. While the owner is looking for the proof, the victim is going through post-exposure prophylaxis for rabies, a series of 7 shots, which is a very painful series of shots. For reference, see Article 50 from the Natick website. https://www.natickma.gov/DocumentCenter/View/15751/Article-50----Police-Regulations
articles.title: Amend Bylaw Article 50, Section 16 (Animal Control)

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to appropriate the Community Preservation Fund annual revenues into each of the Community Preservation Act (CPA) accounts, which include Community (Affordable) Housing, Open Space and Recreation, and Historic Resources; and further appropriate annual revenues for administration and operation for use by the Community Preservation Committee (CPC) in Fiscal Year 2027, in accordance with General Laws Chapter 44B, or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Community Preservation Committee Chair Mr. Bill Ramrage and CED Director Amanda Loomis presented Article 15 to the Finance Committee. The CPC is not bringing forward any specific projects at the Spring 2026 Town Meeting. The appropriation requested under this article is to fund the CPC’s Administration and Operation fund in the amount of 5% of the 110 revenue collected since the last Town Meeting appropriation. The request further specifies that the spending under that request be earmarked for 50% support of a Community Planner position, not to exceed $40,000. The Finance Committee reviewed the general process of the CPA/CPC, as well as the documentation of funding receipts. The committee vote unanimously to support the Article 15 appropriation requests.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to appropriate five percent of the Community Preservation Fund annual revenues in the amount of $73,750 into the Administrative and Operation account to cover Community Preservation Committee (CPC) administrative expenses, of which a portion will be used for the Community Planner salary (Salary) but not to exceed fifty percent of the Salary or $40,000 whichever is less, for the 2027 Fiscal Year, in accordance with MGL c 44B as recommended by the CPC.

articles.article_language: To see what action the Town will take to hear and/or discuss a report of the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee; and to see what action Town Meeting will take to: 1) extend the term of the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee and/or 2) amend the charge of the committee. Or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Mr. Frank Foss and Ms. Grace Keeney presented this article. They gave a brief overview of the anticipated report and presentation. The Finance Committee discussed whether or not the requested extension of term would be sufficient to complete and implement any recommendations. The presenters clarified that their charge includes making recommendations, but does not include implementation and any changes required to facilitate implementation - such as bylaw changes. The presenters felt that the requested extension would be sufficient.
articles.motion_text: Motion A: Move that the town vote to hear and discuss a report of the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee. Motion B: Move that the town vote to extend the term of the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee until the dissolution of 2026 Fall Annual Town Meeting.
open_items: Confirm parsed recommendation, vote quantum, motion, and discussion against the official PDF.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to hear and/or discuss the report of the Natural and Synthetic Turf Field Study Group, or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Select Board member Bruce Evans presented Article 17 to the committee. Mr. Evans explained that the Study Committee has committed to provide Town Meeting with an interim update on the progress made to date. He explained that the Study Committee was continuing the work of fact finding and gathering a broad selection of reports, studies, and other data. No conclusions have been reached, and the Study Committee is interested in updating Town Meeting and soliciting feedback. At the April 7th meeting, the Finance Committee focused on the timeliness of hearing the report, as well as the readiness of the Study Committee to present. Several members felt that the report would be more complete for Fall Town Meeting, and that there aren’t compelling reasons to hear a partial report at this time. Other members were concerned that the presentation hadn’t yet been developed, and were unsure if it would present sufficient facts to warrant the time. Other members felt that an interim report might be valuable to Town Meeting, and that it was a reasonable request that shouldn’t be subjected to unnecessary gate-keeping. A Motion was made to recommend Favorable Action. That Motion received a 7-3-2 vote. However, since a minimum of 8 votes is required to make any recommendation, the Finance Committee has No Recommendation on the subject matter of Article 17.
articles.motion_text: MOVE to hear and discuss the report of the Natural and Synthetic Turf Study Group.
open_items: Confirm parsed recommendation, vote quantum, motion, and discussion against the official PDF.

articles.title: Natural and Synthetic Turf Field Study Group Report

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to approve a resolution commemorating the 250th anniversary of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, and affirming Natick support of the Constitution of the United States, or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Joshua Ostroff presented Article 18. Mr. Ostroff presented this article as both a celebration of the 250th anniversary of the 1776 Natick Resolve for Independence, a Natick Town Meeting resolution supporting the then-pending declaration of independence from England, and a reaffirmation of our of current commitment to the United States Constitution. Further, the article intends to positively affirm the indigenous peoples of the land currently comprising the United States and Natick, to counter the derogatory and negative references to such peoples included in both the declaration of independence and the US Constitution. 117 This article was reviewed and discussed at two separate FinCom meetings. At the initial meeting, certain FinCom members pointed to language in an earlier version of the motion that pointed to our current political climate in a way that such members felt would be divisive. Such language was removed from the version presented above, which was the subject of the second FinCom meeting at which this article was discussed. A FinCom member asked if the sponsor would be willing further amend the motion to remove language stating that Town Meeting “opposes the curtailment of rights embodied in the United States Constitution and its amendments that Town Meeting members have taken an oath to support and uphold” also on the basis of such language potentially being divisive. The sponsor declined. It was noted that although it was unanimously adopted the original 1776 Natick Resolve for Independence was likely divisive. Further, other FinCom members expressed that the language in the current version of the motion “opposing tyranny and injustice” and “opposing the curtailment of rights embodied in the Constitution” ought to be non-controversial given that Town Meeting members are required to pledge allegiance to the Constitution as a condition to taking their elective positions. A member of the public objected to the reference to ‘indigenous’ peoples and expressed preference for the generic phrase ‘all’ peoples. The sponsor declined to make such change. Another member of the public and a FinCom member each noted that both the Declaration of Independence and the US Constitution include disparaging references to indigenous peoples. The sponsor noted that countering such disparaging references are specifically why Article 18 includes language naming and proclaiming respect for such indigenous peoples. Niki Lefebrvre, Ph.D., Executive Director of the Natick Historical Society (which has the original 1776 Natick Resolve for Independence in its collection and has the full text of the resolution available on its website) expressed enthusiastic support for Article 18. She noted that resolves for independence were requested by the Continental Congress and were passed by 58 towns in Massachusetts including Natick. As a 250-year-old document, she noted that the 1776 Natick Resolve for Independence, does include dated and challenging language and assumptions, and she expressed that Town Meeting debate about such language and assumptions would be a fitting tribute to the spirit of the resolve and its resonance in 2026. FinCom voted on a motion to amend the motion for Article 18 to remove the 5th bullet point. The motion failed, receiving only 2 favorable votes. FinCom then voted favorably to recommend that Town Meeting approve the motion for Article 18 as appearing above.
articles.motion_text: Move to adopt the following resolution: Whereas on June 20, 1776, Natick Town Meeting unanimously appro ved the Resolve for Independence, which along with resolutions passed in almost 60 Massachusetts towns and in other colonies presaged and endorsed the Declarat ion of Independence adopted on July 4, 1776 by the Continental Congress, and Whereas the Resolve states in part “we, the inhabitants of Natick, in town meeting assembled, do hereby declare, agreeably to the aforementio ned Resolve, that should the Honorable Continental Congress declare these American Colo nies independent of the Kingdom of Great Britain, we will with our lives and f ortunes join with the other inhabitants of the Colony, and with those of the other Colonies, in su pporting them in said measure, which we look upon to be both important and necessary; and which , if we may be permitted to suggest an opinion, the sooner it is entered into the f ewer difficulties shall we have to conflict with; and the grand object of peace, liberty, and safety, will be more likely speedily to be restored and established in our once happy land,” and 116 Whereas the authors of the Natick Resolve, Rev. Stephen Badger, Captain John Coolidge and Daniel Morse, 4th set their names to this resolution at great personal risk, and Whereas from its earliest days, Natick Town Meeting has recognized the intrinsic bond between self-determination, local government, and the common good of a free people; Now therefore, Natick Town Meeting: ● Commemorates the 250th anniversary of the Natick Resolve for Independence; ● Honors the residents of Natick who in 1776 and the e nsuing years fought for liberty, justice, and independence; ● Acknowledges the contradictions inherent to the American Revolution, wherein colonists sought self-determination and freedom from England even as many practiced enslavement and subjugation; ● Respects the legacy of Indigenous peoples who inhabited the land before the founding of our town and the United States; ● Opposes the curtailment of rights embodied in the Uni ted States Constitution and its amendments that Town Meeting members have taken an oath to support and uphold; ● Opposes tyranny and injustice; and ● Supports life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness. Resolved this 28th day of April, 2026.

articles.purpose: To approve a resolution commemorating the 250th anniversary of the Natick Town Meeting Resolve for Independence, and affirming Natick support of the Constitution of the United States, or otherwise act thereon.
articles.title: Resolution to honor the 1776 Natick Resolve for Independence and to uphold the United States Constitution in 2026
